one_line_takeaway: For first-pass prompt-injection screening, reorganizing a modest (5k-sample) training corpus into geometrically matched positive/negative cells lets a compiled, sub-millisecond linear SVM (92.1% F1, 96.0% recall) substantially outperform an off-the-shelf 22M-parameter Prompt Guard 2 transformer (59.1% F1, 44.4% recall, ~150x slower) on the same holdout, though neither resolves use-versus-mention ambiguity.
key_finding: On the 524-case v5 holdout (248 malicious, 276 benign), the compiled Mirror-trained L1 SVM reaches 0.9207 F1, 0.8848 precision, 0.9597 recall (31 FP, 10 FN; bootstrap 95% CI F1 [0.895,0.943], precision [0.844,0.921], recall [0.934,0.983]) at 0.32ms mean / 0.13ms median / 1.40ms p95 latency. On the identical holdout, Prompt Guard 2 (22M params, off-the-shelf) reaches only 0.5914 F1, 0.8871 precision, 0.4435 recall (14 FP, 138 FN; CI F1 [0.529,0.648], precision [0.828,0.941], recall [0.381,0.506]) at 49.4ms median / 109.2ms mean / 324.4ms p95 latency -- i.e., PG2 is comparably precise but misses the majority of attacks and is roughly two orders of magnitude slower. A 75-pattern regex baseline (L3) reaches 99.2% precision but only 14.1% recall (24.7% F1) on the same set. Under a fixed model family, the v2-to-v3 geometry-only intervention (same corpus reorganized into stricter source-locked cells) moved F1 from 0.835 (356 FP) to 0.918-0.926 F1 (62->50 FP, cutting false positives by over 80%), and a mixed-ratio ablation (v2 corpus, 3 seeds) showed holdout F1 degrading monotonically from 0.837+/-0.005 at 100% Mirror data to 0.788+/-0.002 at 0% Mirror data, with no intermediate blending ratio outperforming pure Mirror. Provenance auditing during v3 development found 18.6% train-eval contamination in the non-mirror baseline and 7% in the early mirror corpus, which had been inflating earlier results before being fixed by content-hash-enforced splitting. On the hard-benign use-versus-mention challenge set, the L1 SVM's false-positive rate is 51.9% at t=0.0 (12.3% even at a raised threshold of +2.0), versus 21.3% FPR for PG2 -- i.e., the semantic model is materially better, though still far from production-ready, at resolving quoted/discussed-attack ambiguity that the sparse lexical model cannot.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'Mirror,' a data-curation design pattern for prompt injection detection corpora: it organizes training data into a matrix of 'cells' indexed by attack reason (8 categories: instruction override, roleplay jailbreak, meta-probe, exfiltration, adversarial suffix, indirect injection, obfuscation, constraint bypass) and language (EN/RU/ZH/AR, 32 cells total), each cell containing matched (mirrored) malicious and benign examples aligned across nuisance dimensions (language, format, length, topic) so a linear classifier cannot exploit corpus shortcuts. Using this curated 5,000-sample corpus (31/32 cells closed with public data), the authors train a sparse character n-gram (n=3..5, min_df=5, max_features=15000) LinearSVC and compile its weights into a static Rust artifact (phf perfect hash map) with no external model runtime, intended as the first-pass (L1) screening layer in a larger layered defense pipeline called Parapet (L0 normalize -> L1 linear screen -> L2a semantic scan -> L3 pattern rules -> L4 multi-turn context). The central empirical claim, demonstrated via a fixed-model-family v2-to-v3 ablation and a stricter v5 multilingual/validity-contract checkpoint, is that strict data geometry (not model scale) is the dominant lever for L1 prompt-injection screening performance.
threat_model: The paper targets control-plane prompt injection attacks against LLM-backed systems: prompts or untrusted payloads that attempt to override instructions, reassign roles/persona, extract hidden system/tool context, smuggle instructions via indirect/tool-mediated data, or otherwise hijack the governing policy of a protected model. It explicitly scopes out content-safety violations (toxicity, copyright, PII, academic dishonesty) as a separate, non-prompt-injection class that should not be conflated with control-plane attacks. The defender is the first-pass (L1) request-path classifier in a multi-layer pipeline (Parapet); the threat model assumes the L1 detector itself sits in the security boundary and therefore must be non-promptable (its own behavior must not be alterable by the text it classifies) -- motivating the choice of a compiled, static linear classifier over a promptable LLM-based detector. Residual threats explicitly acknowledged as out of L1's scope: use-versus-mention ambiguity (quoted/discussed attacks, e.g. security whitepapers and CTF writeups), and paraphrase-based evasion that preserves injection semantics while eliminating lexical n-gram traces.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) the 5,000-sample v5 corpus was the largest the team could source under its public-data validity contract, not evidence that performance has plateaued -- one cell (Russian constraint_bypass) remains an explicit accepted miss (31/32 cells closed) after direct review found only 2 marginal candidates (0.5% hit rate); (2) character n-grams do not resolve contextual/use-versus-mention ambiguity (51.9% FPR on the hard-benign challenge set) and the method has no mechanism for true paraphrase-robust evasion that preserves injection semantics while destroying lexical n-gram traces; (3) the 524-case holdout is described by the authors as 'modest in size,' and broader external validity on unseen production traffic is unproven despite bootstrap confidence intervals; (4) public prompt-injection datasets used for curation are inherently noisy (mixed content-safety/PI labeling, poor provenance, English/family overrepresentation), and Mirror's source contracts mitigate but do not eliminate this; (5) the paper does not show how to retire or shrink the L2a semantic layer -- it strengthens the case for L1 but leaves the broader layered-architecture question open; (6) the three checkpoints (v2/v3/v5) use different corpora and holdouts and are explicitly not intended as a single directly comparable benchmark table, which limits how strongly the absolute headline numbers should be read across versions; (7) the authors note a JailbreakBench-derived challenge slice that initially appeared to show both models plateauing at ~21% recall was later found to consist of direct harmful-content requests rather than prompt injection, and they retract that slice as a PI benchmark -- flagged as a methodological cautionary finding rather than a result.

datasets_used: v2: 19k-sample train corpus / 2.4k-case (2,386-case) holdout, initial Mirror baseline
v3: 19k locked train corpus / 1,875-case holdout, source-locked provenance cleanup, same model family as v2
v5: 5,000-sample curated corpus / 524-case holdout (248 malicious, 276 benign), strict multilingual validity contract, 31/32 of an 8-reason x 4-language cell topology closed with public-source data
Hard-benign adversarial challenge set: 2,386 security-adjacent documents (whitepapers, CTF writeups, red-team documentation) for use-versus-mention robustness testing
JailbreakBench paraphrase-variant slice (used, then retracted as a PI benchmark after post-hoc inspection showed it was direct harmful-content-request material, not prompt injection)
Public source corpora only (open-source/public-data validity contract); ProtectAI's DeBERTa-v3 training recipe cited as the initial data-sourcing starting point before Mirror diverged
quantitative_results: v5 main result: Mirror L1 SVM -- 0.9207 F1, 0.8848 precision, 0.9597 recall, 31 FP, 10 FN on 524-case holdout; bootstrap 95% CI F1 [0.895,0.943], precision [0.844,0.921], recall [0.934,0.983]
v5 latency: L1 SVM 0.32ms mean / 0.13ms median / 1.40ms p95, vs. PG2 (22M) 109.2ms mean / 49.4ms median / 324.4ms p95
v5 PG2 (22M) comparison: 0.5914 F1, 0.8871 precision, 0.4435 recall, 14 FP, 138 FN; CI F1 [0.529,0.648], precision [0.828,0.941], recall [0.381,0.506]
v5 L3 regex baseline (75 hand-written patterns, 10 categories): 99.2% precision, 14.1% recall, 24.7% F1

v2 to v3 geometry ablation (fixed model family): v2 = 0.835 F1 / 0.931 recall / 356 FP (2,386-case holdout); v3 = 0.918 F1 (precision 0.932, recall 0.905, 62 FP, 90 FN, 1,875-case holdout), improving further to 0.926 F1 (precision 0.945, recall 0.908, 50 FP, 87 FN) after further cleaning
Train-eval contamination found during provenance audit: 18.6% in non-mirror baseline, 7% in early mirror corpus
Hard-benign challenge set FPR: L1 SVM 51.9% at t=0.0, 12.3% at raised threshold +2.0; PG2 (22M) 21.3% FPR

v5 cell-closure detail: Russian constraint_bypass left as the single accepted miss (2 marginal candidates found across all sources, 0.5% hit rate); Russian lanes generally required tail-queue review across 444 rows from 10 source-specific queues
Historical ProtectAI DeBERTa-v3 baseline (v2 protocol, 2,386-case holdout, not carried into main v5 table): 0.539 F1, 0.615 precision, 0.480 recall
86M-parameter PG2 variant evaluated separately: recall profile reported as 'nearly identical' to the 22M model despite 4x the parameter count (no exact numbers given in text)
baselines_compared: Prompt Guard 2 22M-parameter model (Meta, off-the-shelf, no fine-tuning; default pg_threshold=0.5, block_threshold=0.5) -- primary head-to-head baseline
Prompt Guard 2 86M-parameter variant (secondary check; recall reported as similar to 22M variant)
ProtectAI DeBERTa-v3-base prompt-injection-v2 classifier (historical v2-protocol comparison only, not in main v5 table)
L3 regex layer: 75 hand-written patterns across 10 attack categories
PromptScreen (cited as concurrent/related work, not directly re-run by the authors): a TF-IDF linear SVM multi-stage pipeline reported in its own paper on a >30,000-prompt corpus
